channel.title: Writing on Ella Goh
channel.description: Recent content in Writing on Ella Goh

item.title: On building small, quiet websites
item.description:
The internet is loud by default. Most pages are trying to keep you scrolling, clicking, or signing up for something. A small, quiet website feels like the opposite of that — almost like walking into a side street café after leaving a busy road.

When I think about what I want from my own site, it’s less about reach and more about tone. I want a place where someone can land, read a few lines, and feel their shoulders drop a little. That means fewer colors, fewer boxes, and enough space around the words so they can breathe.

item.title: Notes on learning slowly
item.description:
Most advice about learning sounds like a race: move fast, consume more, never fall behind. But in real life, the things that stay with me rarely come from rushing. They come from the days when I sit with one idea a little longer than necessary.

Learning slowly doesn’t mean avoiding hard work. It means accepting that depth has a different tempo than urgency. Reading the same paragraph twice can feel inefficient, but sometimes that’s how understanding actually arrives — not as a flash, but as a quiet click after the tenth repetition.

item.title: What I pay attention to online
item.description:
Attention is the real currency online. Every timeline, feed, and notification is a bid for it. If I don’t choose what I care about, something else will choose for me — usually whatever is the loudest.

These days, I try to keep my list of “worth noticing” very small: people I genuinely know, writers who make me think slower, and projects that feel like they were made by hand instead of by committee. That usually means fewer accounts, fewer platforms, and more direct subscriptions.
